Facts.text: Maya ruler of Palenque K'inich Kan Bahlam dedicates the Temple of the Inscriptions to Pakal, his recently deceased father and the city's greatest ruler
Battle of Pontlevoy: one of largest battles of early Medieval France won by Fulk the Black and Hebert I of Maine against Odo II of Blois in the Loire Valley
The Battle of Ménfő takes place in Hungary, won by a German force led by Peter Orseolo over Hungarians
Mindaugas is crowned King of Lithuania
Bohemian religious reformer Jan Hus is burned at the stake in Constance, Germany
Opening ceremony of Kyoto's Daitokuji Shinju at the sub-temple
England and Scotland sign Treaty of Edinburgh
Córdoba, Argentina, is founded by Jerónimo Luis de Cabrera
English admiralFrancis Draketakes Portuguese Forts at Taag
Majesteitsbrief: Emperor Rudolf II grants Bohemia freedom of religion
Dutch governor general John Pieterszoon Coen takes Banda islands, 15,000 die
Swedish troops under Gustaf II Adolf land at Peenemunde
Johan van Walbeeck's ships bypass St-Anna Bay, Curaçao
Battle at La Marfée Sedan: Earl Soisson beats French government army
Fire on Dutch Dam (Amsterdam's city hall burns)
LaSalle leaves Montreal to explore Ohio River
French troops conquer Maastricht as part of the Franco-Dutch War
British General Burgoyne captures Fort Ticonderoga from Americans
British-French sea battle at Negapatam (off India)
US Congress unanimously resolves to name the US currency the "dollar" and adopts decimal coinage
French government proclaims end to stamp/land tax
Orange troops occupy Area at Duurstede
French Revolution: the National Assembly forms a committee of thirty members to write a new constitution
US law makes aliens "liable to be apprehended, restrained, ... & removed as alien enemies"
Battle at Algeciras: French fleet beats British
Christian Hebbel's "Judith" premieres in Berlin
William Wells Brown publishes "Clotel," the first novel by an African American
1st Republican state convention held in Jackson, Michigan
Lyman Blake patents a shoe manufacturing machine
Skirmish at Devall's Bluff, Arkansas (106 casualties)
Battle of Williamsport, Maryland [-Jul 07]
Northern Territory passes from New South Wales to South Australia
Black candidate for Lieutenant Governor of Virginia, Dr J. H. Harris defeated
14 Russian Jews from Bilu arrive in Jaffa, Palestine
William T. Stead's "The Maiden Tribute of Modern Babylon" 1st published, describes how he was able to buy a 13-year-old girl in "The Pall Mall Gazette"
Horlick's of Wisconsin offers 1st malted milk to public
Dadabhai Naoroji elected as first Indian Member of Parliament in Britain
Striking steel workers in Homestead, Pennsylvania, fire on scabs, killing 7
The small town of Pomeroy, Iowa, is nearly destroyed by a tornado that kills 71 people and injures 200
Cleveland sends 2,000 troops to Chicago to suppress Pullman strike
US Senate agrees to annex Hawaii
George Wyman arrives in NYC by motorcycle 51 days out of San Francisco
Two Russian cruisers move into the Red Sea and begin to stop ships of Britain, Germany, and other nations they believe friendly to Japan
Tom Reece takes 5 weeks to compile the highest recorded billiards break in a match (499,135) in London, his 'cradle' cannon method is soon banned
Donald Lippincott runs world record 100m (10.6)
V Summer (Modern) Olympic Games officially open Stockholm, Sweden; events conducted prior to the ceremony dating back to 5 May
The second of four fatal Jersey Shore shark attacks occurs at Spring Lake when Charles Bruder (27) bleeds to death, five days after the first
British R-34 lands in NY, 1st airship to cross Atlantic (108 hr)
New York Yankees score MLB record 14 runs in 5th inning of a 17-0 rout of Washington Senators
Dutch auto/airplane manufacturer Trompenburg declares bankruptcy
Rail crash on New Zealand's main trunk line; 17 killed and 28 injured
First photo is sent experimentally across the Atlantic by radio from the US to England
First all-talking motion picture shown in NY (Lights of New York)
Largest recorded hailstone at the time, weighing 1.5 lbs and measuring 7 inches in diameter, falls in Potter, Nebraska
St. Louis Cardinals set MLB run record with 28 runs on 28 hits (28-6 v Phillies)

Fred Newton (27) commences attempt to swim entire length of the Mississippi River from Ford Dam, Minnesota, to New Orleans, Louisiana; he covers 1826 miles over 176 days, almost twice the 90 days he estimates
Cubs' future All Star shortstop Billy Jurges is shot twice in Chicago hotel room by a spurned girlfriend, Violet Popovich Valli; no charges laid
First-class postage increases to 3 cents from 2 cents
Dutch architect Adriaan van de Steur's Museum Boijmans Van Beuningen opens in Rotterdam
114°F (46°C), Moorhead, Minnesota (state record)
121°F (49°C), Steele, North Dakota (state record)
A major breach of the Manchester, Bolton and Bury Canal in England sends millions of gallons of water cascading 200 feet into the River Irwell
German Nazis close last Jewish enterprises
WWII: German commander Von Hoth's 4th Panzer Army captures the western part of Russian city of Voronezh
2nd day of Battle of Kursk, USSR: Operation Citadel: 25,000 Germans killed
US destroyer William D Porter [Willie Dee] launched

World's largest circus tent catches fire at Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey Circus in Hartford, Connecticut, killing at least 167 people and injuring 700 in one of the worst fire disasters in US history
US President Harry Truman signs executive order establishing Medal of Freedom
Cincinnati Reds catcher Walker Cooper goes 6 for 7, (3 HRs, 3 singles, 5 runs scored, 10 RBI) in 23-4 win v Chicago Cubs
Freak heat wave sent central coast of Portugal to 158°F for 2 minutes
German DR recognizes Oder-Neisse borders with Poland
Last tram ride in London
KMOS TV channel 6 in Sedalia-Warrensburg, MO (PBS) begins broadcasting
Tunisian government of M'zali, resigns
Cleveland outfielder Jim Busby hits a second grand slam on consecutive days during Indians 4-2 win v Kansas City A's
Adolfo López Mateos elected President of Mexico
Saar becomes part of German Federal Republic
WENH TV channel 11 in Durham, NH (PBS) begins broadcasting
Dr Barbara Moore completes a 3,207 mile walk from LA to NYC
Emir Said al-Djazairi takes van Algerian throne in Syria
Nuclear test shot Sedan; part of Operation Plowshare
South African workers' union leader Billy Nair (33) arrested and charged with sabotage and attempting to overthrow the government
Malawi (formerly Nyasaland) declares independence from the United Kingdom
The Beatles' film "A Hard Day's Night" premieres at The Pavilion in London, England
Psychedelic rock band "Jefferson Airplane" forms in San Francisco, California
Malawi becomes a republic, Dr Hastings Kamuzu Banda becomes president
Nigerian Civil War erupts as Nigerian forces invade the secessionist state of Biafra
Sacharov publishes "Manifest of 10,000 words"
Frente Obrero y Liberacion (FOL) forms in Curacao
California passes the first no-fault divorce law in the United States
Irish Minister for External Affairs Partick Hillery pays an unofficial visit to the Falls Road area of Belfast, an areas only just subject to a curfew by British Army
A member of the Official Irish Republican Army (OIRA) is killed in a premature explosion in County Tipperary, Republic of Ireland
Barend Biesheuvel government forms in Netherlands
Dr Hastings Kamuzu Banda sworn in as President for Life of Malawi
White House Plumbers unit formed to plug news leaks
Argentine government falls
Art Wall Jr. at 51 years, 7 months, 10 days becomes the second oldest to win a PGA Tour event, beating Gary McCord by 1 at the Greater Milwaukee Open (oldest Sam Snead)
Comoros declare independence from France (most of them)
Soyuz 21 carries two cosmonauts to Salyut 5 space station
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
A sleeping car catches fire in Taunton, Somerset, killing twelve people
Israeli jet fighters swoop over predominantly Muslim West Beirut
IRA bomb explodes in British consulate in Antwerp
France performs nuclear test
Supreme Court rules retirement plans can't pay women less
New Zealand sisters Liz and Rose Signal become the first twins to play in the same cricket Test match, v England at Headingley
Atlanta infielder Bob Horner becomes 11th player to hit 4 home runs in a MLB game during Braves 11-8 loss v Montreal
Ex-minister Arturo Tolentino failed coup in the Philippines
Premier Nakasone's Liberal Democratic Party wins Japan's election
First of three massacres by Sikh extremists takes place in India
USSR performs nuclear test

"Piper Alpha"  oil drilling platform explosion kills 167 in the North Sea about 190 km (120 miles) north-east of Aberdeen, Scotland; rig operated by Occidental Petroleum (Caledonia) Limited, explodes, 167 die
Carlos Salinas de Gortari elected President of Mexico
US marshals & FCC seize pirate radio station WHOT in Brooklyn
England cricket batsman Graham Thorpe scores 114 on Test debut, in drawn 3rd Test v Australia at Trent Bridge
21.1 inches (51.6 cm) rainfall at Americus, Georgia (state record)
CFL American expansion Shreveport Pirates lose game, 40-10 at the Ottawa Rough Riders; fold 1995
Lerou Burrell runs world record 100m (9.85)
Venezuelan Congress approves the country's first investment law allowing for foreign participation in oil exploration and production
In a 2-0 win v Brewers, New York Yankees closer John Wetteland sets MLB record 20 consecutive saves en route to 24
Australian cricket batsman Steve Waugh completes twin centuries (108 & 116) in 268 run 3rd Test win v England at Old Trafford
England cricket spin bowler Robert Croft saves England from defeat with a famous unbeaten 37 in 190 minutes on day 5 of 2nd Test v South Africa at Old Trafford, Manchester
Hong Kong's Kai Tak Airport is closed and the new Hong Kong International Airport at Chek Lap Kok becomes operational

U.S. Army private Barry Winchell dies from baseball-bat injuries inflicted in his sleep the previous day by fellow soldiers for his relationship with transgendered showgirl and former Navy combat medic, Calpernia Addams
Real estate developer Stan Kroenke announces the acquisition of the Denver Nuggets, Colorado Avalanche and the Pepsi Centre for $450m from the Ascent Entertainment Group
West Ham United FC pays a record £1.8 million for the late Bobby Moore's collection of memorabilia; 79 items, including his 1966 World Cup winner's medal

70-meter Eupatoria Planetary Radar sends a METI message (Cosmic Call 2) to 5 stars: Hip 4872, HD 245409, 55 Cancri, HD 10307 and 47 Ursae Majoris that will arrive in 2036, 2040, May 2044, September 2044, and 2049, respectively
IOC awards London the right to host the Games of the XXX Olympiad in 2012 with a vote of 54 to 50 over Paris on the fourth and final ballot.
The Nathula Pass between India and China, sealed during the Sino-Indian War, re-opens for trade after 44 years
Jadranka Kosor becomes the first female Prime Minister of Croatia
Gunmen kill 18 people in Turbat, Pakistan
14 people are killed after a train runs over a rickshaw in Sheikhupura, Pakistan
3 people are killed and 181 are injured after a Boeing 777 crash lands at San Francisco Airport
42 people are killed and 5 missing after a freight train carrying crude oil rolls unmanned for 12km before derailing at Lac-Megantic, Quebec igniting fires and explosions
42 people are killed in an attack on a boarding school in Mamudo, Nigeria
British & Irish Lions Rugby Union team thrash Australia, 41-16 in Sydney for a 2-1 series win, their first against the Wallabies since 1997
Israeli Air Force strike kills 7 Hamas militants in the Gaza Strip
African American Alton Sterling is shot by Louisiana police in Baton Rouge, while being restrained on the ground, the killing is filmed
African American Philando Castile is shot by police in St Paul, Minnesota after being pulled over for a broken rear light, killing is filmed
Pokémon Go, the real-world mobile game by Niantic, is first released
South African athlete Oscar Pistorius is sentenced to 6 years in jail for the murder of his girlfriend Reeva Steenkamp in 2013
France announces it will ban petrol and diesel cars by 2040
Illinois State Senate votes to override Governor Bruce Rauner's veto to approve 1st budget in more than two years, longest-lasting such dispute of any state
Sri Lankan government confirms outbreak of dengue fever has killed at least 227
Floods and landslides begin in south-western Japan, killing at least 200 people with more missing. Evacuation orders for nearly 2 million.
Iranian instagram star Maedeh Hojabri confesses on state TV for posting videos of her dancing
Paraguay announces it has eliminated malaria
Prime Minister Carlos Agostinho do Rosário of Mozambique sacks the board of Lam Airlines after he is left stranded when a plane runs out of fuel
Jorge Masvidal (34-13) sets a UFC record by needing just 5 seconds to KO Ben Askren (19-1, 1 NC) during their welterweight bout at UFC 239 in Las Vegas, Nevada
Super Rugby Final, Christchurch: Canterbury Crusaders win their 10th title with a 19-3 win over Argentina's Jaguares
America officially begins withdrawing from the World Health Organization
Zoonotic diseases, which jump from animals to humans, are increasing due to unsustainable farming and climate change according to new report by the UN
First ever joint appearance by MI5 head Ken McCallum and the FBI Director Christopher A. Wray, warning China is the "biggest long-term threat to our economic and national security"
Rocco Morabito, Italian second-most wanted and one of the world's most power drug brokers extradited from Brazil to Italy, after being on the run for 28 years
Black woman Sonya Massey shot and killed in her home in Springfield, Illinois, by sheriff deputy Sean Grayson, who is later charged with first-degree murder
Suriname parliament elects Jennifer Geerlings-Simons as the country's first female president
Richard the Lionheartis crowned King of England, upon thedeath of King Henry II
Richard IIIis crowned King of England after deposingEdward V
Battle of Fornovo, near Parma: French KingCharles VIIIbeats Holy League
ConquistadorHernán Cortésgranted lands and title of Marquisate of the Oaxaca Valley by Spanish KingCharles V
Pacificatie of Boulogne: KingCharles IXsigns edict ending the French War of Religion peace treaty with Huguenots
Battle at Sedgemoor: KingJames IIdefeats the Duke of Monmouth
Pirate CaptainWilliam Kiddis captured in Boston

Battle of Wagram: After a two day battle,Napoleon's French and allied army decisively defeats the Austrian army underArchduke Charles, leads to the breakup of the Austrian and British-led Fifth Coalition against France
National Black convention meets in Rochester NY, ex-slaveFrederick Douglassattends
Louis Pasteursuccessfully gives an anti-rabies vaccine to 9-year-old Joseph Meister, saving his life
Jose Rizal forms political group La Liga Filipina in Manila
The US Democratic Party nominates little known New York judge Alton B. Parker for presidential nominee - virtually assuring the election ofTheodore Roosevelt
Alfred Deakinbecomes Prime Minister of Australia for the second time
American explorerRobert Pearysets out of his eighth arctic expedition sailing from NYC for the north pole
T. E. Lawrencecaptures port of Aqaba from Turks
Institute for Sexual Science opened in Berlin by physicianMagnus Hirschfeldto establish study of sexual science
"Nertsery Rhymes" short film starring Ted Healy and His Stooges premieres, one of the first film appearance ofThe Three Stooges
New York Yankees team unveils a monument to former captainLou Gehrigin center field at Yankee Stadium; the future Hall of Famer died the previous month
Anne Frank's family goes into hiding in After House, Amsterdam
French GeneralCharles de Gaullearrives in Washington, D.C.
US GeneralGeorge S. Pattonlands in France
Abbott andCostello's film "The Naughty Nineties" released; features longest version of their "Who's on First?" routine
Washington SenatorRick Ferrellcatches a record 1,722 games
Spain votes forFrancisco Francoas Head of State for life until death
TheAK-47goes into production in the Soviet Union
MLB CommissionerFord Frickinaugurates Cy Young Award, to honor baseball's outstanding pitcher of the season
Harry S. TrumanLibrary is dedicated in Independence, Missouri

John Lennon(16) andPaul McCartney(15) meet for the first time as Lennon's rock group, the Quarrymen, perform at St. Peter's Woolton Parish Church in Liverpool; mutual friend Ivan Vaughn introduces them
New York Yankees 'M&M boys'Roger Maris&Mickey Mantlehit 2 HRs each in 7-5 win v Twins, Mantle his 4th consecutive homer
"Ned Kelly" starringMick Jaggerbegins filming in and around Braidwood, New South Wales, Australia
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes Sonate for alto, opus 147
MLB Philadelphia Phillies starterSteve Carltonfans 7 Cardinals to reach 2,836 strikeouts, the most by a left-handed pitcher in MLB history
Ex-Phillies 3rd basemanMike Schmidtbecomes the first retired MLB player to be named to start the All Star game; he elects not to play
"Jetsons the Movie" byHanna-BarberawithTiffany Darwish, premieres
MLB CommissionerFay Vincentinstitutes plans to realign NL
John F. Kennedy Jr, gives notice of quitting as ADA in Manhattan
"Forrest Gump", directed by Robert Zemeckis and starringTom Hanks,Robin Wright, andGary Sinise, is released (Academy Awards Best Picture 1995)
Montreal Expos retire Baseball Hall of Fame outfielderAndre Dawson's uniform #10
Wimbledon Women's Tennis:Serena Williamsbeats older sister Venus 7-6, 6-3 for her first Wimbledon singles title
Martina Navratilovaclaims her 20th all-time Wimbledon title as she and Leander Paes beat Andy Ram & Anastassia Rodionova 6-3, 6-3 in the mixed doubles final
"Incubus" singerBrandon Boydannounces the release of his debut solo album, "The Wild Trapeze"
Floyd Mayweather Jr.is stripped of his WBO welterweight boxing title after failing to pay $200k sanctioning fee and vacate his 2 junior middleweight titles
Chilcot Report, UK's Iraqi War Inquiry released; concludesTony Blairoverstated case for war and was unpreparaed
LA Clippers acquire 2 NBA megastars in one day; Finals MVPKawhi Leonardfrom Toronto in free agency, and Paul George from OKC for an unprecedented trade bounty of players and picks
Kansas City Chiefs quarterbackPatrick Mahomesagrees to the largest contract for an athlete in sports history, inking a 12-year deal that could end up being worth US$503 million
Mary Simon is named Canada's first indigenous Governor General by Prime MinisterJustin Trudeau
Dramatic day in British politics as more than 40 government ministers and aides withdraw their support from PMBoris Johnsonand resign, a 24 hour record
Louis ArmstrongHouse Museum opens new archive facility, the Louis Armstrong Center, in Queens, NYC
Papal bull of Pope Clement VI issued during the Black Death states Jews are not to blame and encourages their protection
Congress issues the "Declaration of the Causes and Necessity of Taking up Arms", written by Thomas Jefferson andJohn Dickinsonand lists American grievances but denies any intent to be independent
American Declaration of Independence announced on front page of "PA Evening Gazette"
The Central Executive Committee accepts the Treaty of Union, signed in Moscow in December 1922, and the Russian Empire becomes the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics
Nicaragua becomes the first nation to ratify the United Nations Charter
Johann Stobäus, North German composer, born in Graudenz, Royal Prussia, Crown of the Kingdom of Poland (d. 1646)
Albert Schop, German composer, born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1667)
Pietro Reggio, Italian composer, singer, and lutenist, born in Genoa, Republic of Genoa (d. 1685)
Gerrit Berckheyde, Dutch painter, born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1698)
Pieter Burman the Elder, Dutch classical scholar, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1741)
Nicola Francesco Haym, Italian composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1729)
Antoine de Jussieu, French naturalist, born in Lyon, France (d. 1758)
Franz Anton Maichelbeck, German organist, composer, kapellmeister (Freiburg Cathedral), and priest, born on Reichenau Island, Prince-Bishopric of Constance, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1750)
Daniel Morgan, American soldier and politician (Battle of Cowpens, Whiskey Rebellion), born in Hunterdon County, New Jersey (d. 1802)
Friedrich Wilhelm Rust, German violinist and composer, born in Wörlitz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1796)
Coelestin Jungbauer, German composer, born in Grattersdorf, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1823)
John Paul Jones, Scottish-American naval captain and the United States first well-known naval hero ("I have not yet begun to fight"), born in Arbigland, Kirkcudbrightshire, Scotland (d. 1792)
Johannes Henricus Midderigh, Dutch wine merchant, politician, and chair of the Second Assembly in the coup d'état, born in Rotterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1800)[1]
John Flaxman, English Neoclassical sculptor (Westminster Abbey tomb stones), born in York, England (d. 1826)
Alexander Wilson, Scottish-American ornithologist and naturalist, born in Paisley, Scotland (d. 1813)
Wenzel Thomas Matiegka, Czech guitarist and composer, baptized in Choceň, Kingdom of Bohemia, Habsburg Empire (d. 1830)
Luis Brión, Curacao military officer who fought in the Venezuelan War of Independence, born in Plantage Rozentak, Curaçao (d. 1821)
William Jackson Hooker, English botanist and director (Kew Gardens), born in Norwich, England (d. 1865)
María Isabella of Spain, Queen of the Two Sicilies (1825-30), born in Royal Palace of Madrid, Spain (d. 1848)
Jacob de Kempenaer, Dutch lawyer and Minister of Internal Affairs (1848-49), born in Amsterdam (d. 1870)
Justus McKinstry, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1897)
Albert von Kölliker, Swiss anatomist and histologist (one of the first to interpret tissue structure in terms of cellular elements), born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1905)
Adolf Anderssen, German chess player (International Tournament 1851, 62), born in Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1879)
Ernst Wilhelm von Brücke, German physician and physiologist, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1892)
Edward Pettus, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Athens, Alabama (d. 1907)
Władysław Żeleński, Polish pianist, organist, and composer, born in Grodkowice, Congress Poland (d. 1921)
Vatroslav Jagić, Croatian scholar of Slavic studies, born in Varaždin, Austrian Empire (d. 1923)
Ángela Peralta, Mexican operatic soprano, harpist, pianist, and composer, born in Mexico City (d. 1883)
John Albert Delany, Australian composer, born in Ratcliff, London (d. 1907)
Edward Anseele, Belgian socialist politician, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1938)
Verner von Heidenstam, Swedish poet and novelist (Charles Men, Nobel 1916), born in Olshammar, Örebro County, Sweden (d. 1940)
Alberto Nepomuceno, Brazilian composer and conductor (Artemis), born in Fortaleza, State of Ceará, Brazil (d. 1920)
Emile Jaques-Dalcroze, Swiss musician, composer, and pedagogue (Dalcroze Eurythmics), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1950)
Princess Victoria of the United Kingdom, fourth child and second daughter of Edward VII and Alexandra of Denmark, born in Marlborough House, London (d. 1935)
David Stanley Smith, American composer (Merrymount), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1949)
Niceto Alcalá-Zamora, 1st Prime Minister of the Second Spanish Republic and President of Spain (1931-36), born in Priego de Cordoba, Spain (d. 1949)
Eino Leino, Finnish poet, born in Paltamo, Finland (d. 1926)
Josef Winckler, German dentist and writer (Quadriga, Pumpernickel), born in Bentlage, Rheine (d. 1966)
Ralph Morgan [Wupperman], American actor (Creeper, Imposter, Jack London), born in New York City (d. 1956)
André Dunoyer de Segonzac, French painter and cartoonist, born in Boussy-Saint-Antoine, France (d. 1974)
Harold Vanderbilt, American yachtsman and America's Cup winner (1930, 34, 37), born in Oakdale, New York (d. 1970)
Willem Dudok, Dutch architect (town hall Hilversum/Beehive Rotterdam), born in Amsterdam (d. 1974)
Ernst Busch, German field marshal (WWII), born in Essen, German Empire (d. 1945)
Annette Kellermann, Australian swimmer, silent-film actress (A Daughter of the Gods; Venus Of The South Seas), and author, born in Marrickville, New South Wales, New South Wales (d. 1975)
Andy Sandham, Englishcricket batsman(14 Tests; 1st triple centurion 325 v WI 1930), born in London, England (d. 1982)
Willy Coppens, Belgian WW I fighter ace, born in Watermael-Boitsfort, Brussels, Belgium (d. 1986)
Thomas W. McKnew, American Vice President, Chairman, and Chairman Emeritus of the National Geographic Society (1954-90), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1990)[1][2]
Richard Krautheimer, American art historian and Byzantinist, born in Fürth, Germany (d. 1994)
Hanns Eisler, Austrian composer (East German National Anthem), born in Leipzig, German Empire (d. 1962)
Frederica Sagor Maas, American playwright and screenwriter, born in New York City (d. 2012)
(Axel) Hugo Theorell, Swedish biochemist (Nobel Prize for Medicine or Physiology, 1955, for discovery of oxidoreductase enzymes), born in Linköping, Sweden (d. 1982)
Erik Wickberg, 9th Salvation Army general, born in Gävle, Sweden (d. 1996)
Robert Whitney, American conductor (Louisville Orchestra, 1937-67), educator (University of Louisville, 1956-71), and composer, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1986)
Elisabeth Lutyens, English composer, born in London (d. 1983)
Léonce Grass, Flemish singer and conductor, born in Borgerhout, Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1993)
Dorothy Kirsten, American operatic soprano (Time to Sing, Chevy Show), born in Montclair, New Jersey (d. 1992)
LaVerne Andrews, American pop and swing jazz singer (The Andrews Sisters - "Boogie Woogie Bugle Boy"; "Rum and Coca-Cola"), born in Mound, Minnesota (d. 1967)
Heinrich Harrer, Austrian mountaineer and author (Seven Years in Tibet), born in Hüttenberg, Austria-Hungary (d. 2006)
Viola Desmond, Canadian businesswoman and civil rights activist (first woman on a Canadian banknote), born in Halifax, Nova Scotia (d. 1965)[1]
Marcel Quinet, Belgian pianist, and composer (Vague et Sillon), born in Binche, Belgium (d. 1986)
Unica Zürn, German writer and artist, born in Berlin (d. 1970)
Arthur Lydiard, New Zealand running coach, born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 2004)
Hugo Cole, British composer and music critic, born in London (d. 1995)
Hugo Yarnold, English cricketer (Worcestershire keeper & Test umpire), born in Worchester, England (d. 1974)
Bert Voeten, Dutch journalist and poet (Crossing), born in Breda, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Eugene List, American concert pianist and teacher (Eastman School of Music), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1985)
Sebastian Cabot, British actor (Kismet; Family Affair - "Mr. French", Time Machine), born in London, England (d. 1977)
Oswaldo Guayasamín, Ecuadorian master painter and sculptor, born in Quito, Ecuador (d. 1999)
César Rodríguez Álvarez, Spanish soccer forward (12 caps; Barcelona) and manager (Zaragoza, Barcelona), born in León, Spain (d. 1995)
Francisco Moncion, American dancer, born in La Vega, Dominican Republic (d. 1995)
William Schallert, American actor (Patty Duke Show - "Martin"; Get Smart - "The Admiral"), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2016)
Cathy O'Donnell [Ann Steely], American film noir actress (Miniver Story, Man from Laramie), born in Siluria, Alabama (d. 1970)
Marie McDonald, American singer and actress known as "The Body Beautiful" (Promises, Promises), born in Burgin, Kentucky (d. 1965)
Louie Bellson, American jazz drummer and orchestra leader (Pearl Bailey Show), born in Rock Falls, Illinois (d. 2009)
Robert Michael White, American test pilot (X-15), born in New York City (d. 2010)
Huub Jacobse, Dutch politician and member of Dutch 2nd chamber (Liberal), born in Zeist, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Merv Griffin, American TV host (Merv Griffin Show), born in San Mateo, California (d. 2007)
Ruth Cracknell, Australian actress (Mother and Son), born in Maitland (d. 2002)
Conrad "Nicky" Hilton Jr., American hotel heir and 1st husband of Elizabeth Taylor (1950-51), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1969)
Alan Leslie "Fluff" Freeman, Australian-born British disc jockey and radio personality (Pick of the Pops), born in New South Wales, Australia (d. 2006)
Charles Whittenberg, American classical composer (Triptych for Brass), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1984)
Jan Hein Donner, Dutch chess grand master, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1988)
Nilo Soruco, Bolivian singer-songwriter ("Ya la pagarán"; "La Caraqueña"), born in Tarija, Bolivia (d. 2004)
Pat Paulsen, American comedian (The Smothers Brothers Comedy Hour), actor, and frequent, presidential candidate, born in South Bend, Washington (d. 1997)
Susan Cabot, American actress (Sorority Girl, Carnival Rock), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1986)
Gerd Zacher, German composer, born in Meppen, Germany (d. 2014)
Françoise Mallet-Joris, Belgian writer (Signs & Wonders), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 2016)
George Armstrong, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame centre (Stanley Cup 1961–62, 1962–63, 1963–64, 1966–67 Toronto Maple Leafs; 7 x NHL All Star), born in Bowlands, Ontario (d. 2021)
Hans-Jürgen Heise, German writer, born in Bublitz (d. 2013)
Danny White, American singer and bandleader, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1996)
Donal Donnelly, Irish actor (The Godfather: Part III, Dead), born in Bradford, West Riding of Yorkshire, England (d. 2010)
Emily Nasrallah, Lebanese writer and women's rights activist (Birds of September, A Cat’s Diary), born in Kfeir, Lebanon (d. 2018)

Jean Campeau, French Canadian businessman (CEO of Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec, 1980-90), and politician (Ministry of Finance, 1971-79; Member of the National Assembly of Quebec, 1994-98), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2025)
László Tábori, Hungarian middle/long distance runner (1,500m world record 1955), born in Košice, Slovakia (d. 2018)
Big Dee Irwin [DiFosco Ervin], American R&B singer ("Swinging on a Star" with Little Eva), and songwriter ("What Kind of Boy"), born in Harlem, New York City (d. 1995)
Della Reese [Delloreese Patricia Early], American singer and actress (Della Reese Show; The Royal Family), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2017)
Frank Austin, English footballer, born in Stoke-on-Trent, England
